text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic low anterior resection for a recto sigmoid cancer in a young male. And he was worked up in a multidisciplinary fashion and elected to undergo a sigmoid resection.

Hello, I'm sharing a robotic low anterior resection for a recto sigmoid cancer in a young male. This was found on a index colonoscopy for rectal bleeding, was at around a twenty five centimeters and above the peritoneal reflection. And he was worked up in a multidisciplinary fashion and elected to undergo a sigmoid resection.

In this case, I believe there were fifty-six lymph nodes, eighteen of which were positive. The patient is getting set up for adjuvant therapy at this time.

The surgeon decides against transrectal extraction due to the large size of the cancer and bulky mesentery, opting instead for an intracorporeal anastomosis. After completing the transection, the specimen is moved aside to perform a sutured anastomosis.

The surgeon places a 60mm stapler distally in the pelvis, requiring two firings to secure the tissue. The stapler fits into the pelvic space despite initial concerns about its size.

The surgeon moves the specimen to the side and checks the bowel length in steep Trendelenburg position. The proximal bowel is rotated clockwise to align the staple lines vertically. The anti-mesenteric side of the sigmoid is exposed for suturing, and the posterior rows of the anastomosis are joined.

The surgeon rotates the proximal bowel clockwise to align the staple lines vertically, exposing the anti-mesenteric side of the sigmoid colon. Using the left hand to assist in rotating the bowel counterclockwise, the surgeon clears pericolic fat and makes a colotomy, followed by a proctotomy.

The surgeon uses their left hand to rotate the bowel counterclockwise, aligning the staple lines for the anastomosis. The colotomy is made, with pericolic fat being cleared away, followed by the proctotomy.

The surgeon begins suturing the inner row in the posterior midline, moving towards the patient's left. ICG is used to document the vascularization of the rectal and colon wall bridges between the staple line and the cut edge of the bowel, confirming adequate blood supply.

The surgeon prepares to switch to a canal technique, focusing on suturing across the anterior aspect of the inner row. The mucosa is visible, and the surgeon aims to prevent it from pouching outward during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies a knot in the suture that is difficult to undo, terminates the problematic suture, and brings the original suture around to the left to connect with the cut suture.
The surgeon encounters a knot that is difficult to undo, decides to terminate the current suture, and brings the original suture back around to the cut suture on the left.

The surgeon performs additional suturing near the end of the anastomosis to achieve hemostasis, addressing a minor bleeding issue. Extra throws are made to prevent the formation of an expanding hematoma in the mesentery.

The surgeon addresses bleeding near the end of the anastomosis by placing additional sutures to achieve hemostasis, preventing an expanding hematoma in the mesentery. The original suture from the back row is then used to canal over the top, imbricating the inner suture line.
The surgeon ties the two suture lines together, then takes the original suture from the back row and canals it over the top to imbricate the inner suture line, enhancing the anastomosis.

Now we'll do our leak test. I do this with a nineteen millimeter rigid scope. Everything looked good. Just cleaning things up, making sure I don't feel uncomfortable about my tension. Patient did great. Discharge on post-operative day one. On to adjuvant therapy. Comments and suggestions are welcome.
